items.text: CNC cut Baltic Birch plywood, epoxy, rubber feet, 3D prints, paint, 18" subwoofer components, lifting hardware

I wanted to see if I could create a minimalist approach to a subwoofer. Creating a simple box with large radius round-over edges, responding to the soft form of the subwoofer shape. The result is a monochrome SUB.

elements.content: My name is Colby I am a Vancouver-based industrial designer with a multidisciplinary background in art, fabrication, and prototyping. I graduated from Emily Carr University in 2024 with a Bachelor of Fine Arts, and since then I have worked with private clients while continuing to develop independent projects and entrepreneurial ventures.

My practice is centered on the design and development of functional objects, with a particular interest in material processes, mechanical systems, and iterative prototyping. I approach design as both a creative and technical discipline, combining conceptual thinking with a strong understanding of how products are manufactured, tested, and refined. My interests evolved from artistic exploration in painting and sculpture toward designing functional objects. Through experimentation with materials, prototyping, and hands-on making, I developed a strong interest in industrial design and the process of bringing ideas into physical form.

items.text: Water misting system, Aluminum Extrusion, Water, Water Pump, 4x6 Fir lumber, Epoxy, Sand, Black paint, pond liner, Plywood, Casted resin feet, Stainless steel Marine hardware

“Pathway to Heaven” is an architectural installation incorporating Sound, water, light and time. The experience allows viewers to bow their heads to step into an elevated platform sitting above a pool of water within a semipermeable space. By bringing an outdoor experience indoors and allowing it to be observed in a controlled setting it allows for the blurring between the boundaries between the natural and the artificial. The pavilion is both a multi sensory experience as well as a sculpture. Not being trained in architecture has allowed me to interpret residential and commercial buildings through the lens of an artist and designer. Studying the works of Mies Van Der Rohe, Valerio Olgiati, Tadao Ando, Peter Zumthor. This approach has allowed me to prioritize form over function as opposed to traditional approach to function over form. Living in Vancouver has made me accustomed to rain and its treatment of objects and surfaces. As rain comes down on the asphalt it glistens a deep black. Making the environment shiny and in constant movement as water flows through the path of least resistance. This state of constant movement is the point of stillness. “Movement is the foundation of stillness”- LAO TZU

The Clients brief was simple. Design, prototype and build the world's lightest camping Chair. I started by creating a frame from carbon fiber tubes and building the design from there, the internal Aluminum hub acts as the pivot point for the tubes making the assembly of the chair almost Instantaneous. Just simply pivot out the legs and the two arms for the saddle chair. The chair Fabric consists of a Dyneema shell, with a protective outer coating keeping it very light weight while also having high abrasion resistance.

Colby (B.2001) is a Vancouver-based industrial designer with a multidisciplinary background in art, fabrication, and prototyping. He graduated from Emily Carr University in 2024 with a Bachelor of Fine Arts, and since then has worked with private clients while continuing to develop independent projects and entrepreneurial ventures.

His practice is centered on the design and development of functional objects, with a particular interest in material processes, mechanical systems, and iterative prototyping. He approaches design as both a creative and technical discipline, combining conceptual thinking with a strong understanding of how products are manufactured, tested, and refined. His interests evolved from artistic exploration in painting and sculpture toward designing functional objects. Through experimentation with materials, prototyping, and hands-on making, he developed a strong interest in industrial design and the process of bringing ideas into physical form.

Over years of hands-on project development, Colby has built experience across a range of fabrication methods, including CNC machining, 3D printing, woodworking, and welding. This broad technical foundation allows him to move fluidly between idea generation, prototyping, and production-minded problem solving. His work is driven by a commitment to creating objects that are clear in purpose, refined in form, and grounded in functionality. He is especially interested in designing products and systems that balance usability, material intelligence, and visual restraint.

For work related inquiries please contact info@colbyonline.com

Additional work available on request.
Website Design and Development: Colby Watton

ABS 3D printed plastic, Stailess Steel fitting, Glycerin smoke device

Glycerin Mask was a design exploration in creating an object that functions both as a wearable sculpture and an experiential object. The idea of the Glycerin Mask was to create a set of goggles that could emit a field of smoke to encamp the face of the model wearing the glasses as he walks down the runway. The object had to be both functional for the model to allow him/her to see while also creating an even layer of smoke across the surface of the glasses.

A Topological Optimization of surface cut holes was created along the main front surface goggles to allow for an even distribution of smokes along the surface leading to a very appealing and interesting effect as the model moves their head. 

In order to create this object that has an internal cavity in the middle section while also creating it from one piece a 3D printer was used to make the front face piece. The piece was then smoothed using a process known as acetone smoothing to get the desired glossy finish I was looking for.
First working prototype on my face. shape and form of the mask fits the form of my face.

The goal for this project was to create a timeless and minimal yet comfortable Church Pew that has a soft approachable feel and look for a local church in Whistler, BC. 

Manufacturing Limitations: Must be able to be manufactured by a local  Whistler Woodworking shop. Hidden fastening techniques must be used in order to hide the appearance of any traditional fastener. Wooden dominoes and mortise and tenon joints to create a seamless design

The final dimensions will be 14ft long and will comprise of 11 rows, 22 individual pews. This project in currently still in progress and will be finished by the end of 2026
3D printed filament, Nema 14 stepper motor, skate board wheels, M6 bolts, 5/16” + nuts, power supply, 3d printed Rubber tpu wheel

Using a Machined Aluminum plate with a standard M6 bolt hole pattern AKA “CheeseBoard” I designed and manufactured a motorized dolly that seamlessly rides along 1.5” diameter tubing. Using a Nema 14 stepper motor and a Nema driver. I 3D printed a mounting block for the Nema stepper motor as well as an enclosure for the stepper driver and power supply. 

 I then designed a 4 point Skate board wheel mounting block to allow for multiple points of contact as it rides the aluminum tube. After prototyping and assembling everything. I then experimented with different variations of 3d printed rubber wheels to softly grip the aluminum tubing allowing it to move along the tube. Having this dolly allows me to get seamless video shots at a constant rpm that can be altered for the desired shot with the speed controller.

Using it in combination with a Gimbal allows me to get incredibly interesting linear motion shots a human could not get simply with a gimbal alone. As well as an ability to attach a camera directly to the aluminum plate to get seamless push-in / out shots.

cells.caption: Giimbal mounted to Motorized Camera Dolly
Under side of Camera Dolly with another gimbal mounting point
